frameworks.name: Revenue Lever Hierarchy
frameworks.description: Revenue has four levers. Most operators pull the hardest lever (more customers) while ignoring easier ones. Sequence by effort-to-impact ratio.
steps: Lever 1 — Price: increase price on existing offer to existing customers. Zero acquisition cost. Highest ROI lever. Test 10-20% increase.
Lever 2 — Retention: keep existing customers longer. Acquiring a new customer costs 5-7x retaining an existing one.
Lever 3 — Expansion: sell more to existing customers (upsell, cross-sell, adjacent offers).
Lever 4 — Acquisition: new customers. Highest cost, longest cycle, most effort.
Audit: what percentage of revenue growth effort is on Lever 4 vs. Levers 1-3?
Rebalance toward highest-ROI lever before increasing acquisition spend
antipatterns: Defaulting to acquisition when retention is the actual constraint
Underpricing out of fear rather than market evidence — most service providers are underpriced
No expansion revenue strategy — leaving known-good customers at single purchase point

frameworks.name: Offer Staircase
frameworks.description: A single offer at a single price point leaves most revenue potential unrealized. A staircase captures buyers at every commitment level and naturally upgrades them over time.
steps: Free tier: builds audience and trust. Demonstrates competence. Entry point for the relationship.
Low-ticket ($7-$97): breaks the payment threshold. A buyer at $10 is categorically different from a non-buyer.
Core offer ($300-$2,000): primary revenue driver. Where most transformation happens.
High-ticket ($3,000-$25,000): premium outcome with premium support. 1-5% of buyers, disproportionate revenue.
Map your current offers to this staircase — identify missing rungs
Design the upgrade path: what naturally leads a buyer from one rung to the next?
antipatterns: Single price point — captures only buyers ready for that commitment level
High-ticket with no low-ticket entry — forces cold buyers to make large commitments
No designed upgrade path — customers plateau at first purchase

frameworks.name: Pricing Power Development
frameworks.description: Pricing power is the ability to raise prices without losing customers. It is built through positioning, not negotiation. Operators with pricing power earn more doing less.
steps: Quantify your value: what is the economic outcome you deliver? Price as a fraction of that, not as cost-plus.
Specialize to reduce comparison: generic providers compete on price; specialists set price.
Build a body of evidence: case studies, testimonials, outcomes data make price defensible
Raise prices on new clients first — test market tolerance without risking existing relationships
Eliminate price-sensitive clients progressively — they consume the most time and generate the most friction
Reframe from time-based to outcome-based pricing — hourly rate caps earnings; outcome pricing does not
antipatterns: Competing on price in a commodity market — a race to the bottom with no floor
Time-based pricing for expertise — caps income at hours available
Discounting to close — attracts price-sensitive clients and signals weak positioning

prompts.name: Revenue Lever Audit
prompts.template: Audit my revenue levers. Current revenue: [amount]. Revenue from price increases in last 12 months: [amount or none]. Churn rate: [%]. Expansion revenue (upsell/cross-sell): [amount]. New customer acquisition spend: [amount]. Identify which lever has the highest untapped ROI and give me the specific action to pull it.

prompts.name: Offer Staircase Design
prompts.template: Design my offer staircase. Current offers: [list with prices]. Target customer: [describe]. Map my existing offers to the staircase tiers, identify missing rungs, and design the upgrade path from entry to high-ticket.

prompts.name: Pricing Power Assessment
prompts.template: Assess my pricing power. Current pricing: [describe — hourly/project/retainer]. Specialization level: [describe]. Evidence of outcomes: [describe]. Last price increase: [when]. Identify the barriers to raising prices and the highest-leverage action to increase pricing power in 90 days.

examples.title: Service Business Price Increase
examples.context: Copywriter charging $75/hour, fully booked, no capacity to take more clients
examples.diagnosis: Fully booked at current price = demand exceeds supply = underpriced
examples.intervention: Raised rate to $125/hour for new clients. Lost 2 of 8 existing clients on renewal. Net revenue increased 40% with 25% less time.

vocabulary.definition: The four mechanisms for growing revenue: price increase, retention improvement, expansion revenue, and new customer acquisition.
Additional revenue from existing customers via upsell, cross-sell, or renewal at higher tier. Highest ROI revenue category.
A tiered sequence of offers from free to ultra-premium, designed to capture buyers at every commitment level and facilitate natural upgrades.
The ability to raise prices without proportionate customer loss. Built through specialization, positioning, and demonstrated outcomes.
A client who selected you primarily on price. Typically generates highest friction, lowest margins, and weakest loyalty.
Pricing tied to the value of the result delivered rather than time spent. Removes the income ceiling inherent in time-based models.

sources.title: $100M Offers
sources.relevance: Offer design, value equation, staircase architecture

sources.title: The Win Without Pitching Manifesto
sources.relevance: Pricing power for service providers, specialist positioning

master_laws: Law 11 (Leverage — pricing is highest-leverage revenue lever)
Law 18 (Reality — market pricing is data, not feeling)
